common_name: Flash Collards (Fast-Maturing Tender Smooth-Leaf Collard Hybrid)

planting.note: Direct sow 10–12 weeks before the first frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Flash is the fastest-maturing collard option, useful for late-season planting when the window for establishing standard 75–80 day varieties has closed.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Flash is a fast-maturing collard hybrid reaching harvest in only 55–65 days from sowing — significantly faster than traditional open-pollinated collard varieties (70–90 days), making Flash particularly valuable for late-season zone 9 plantings where the window for establishing slow-maturing varieties has closed. The smooth, dark-green leaves are more tender and less coarse than the thick leaves of older collard varieties — the faster maturity corresponds with a younger, more tender leaf stage at harvest, making Flash better suited to raw preparations and quick sautés where tenderness is important. Flash has the same excellent cold-hardiness as other collards — exposure to light frost sweetens the leaves by converting starches to sugars, improving flavor quality through the Gulf South winter. As a hybrid variety, Flash cannot be saved for seed but produces reliable, vigorous, uniform plants each season from purchased seed. The faster maturity also allows two consecutive collard plantings within a Gulf South fall season — a first planting in mid-August for October–November harvest and a second planting in late September for December–January harvest. Excellent disease tolerance reduces the need for preventive fungicide applications in the humid Gulf South fall garden.

common_name: Sweet Million Cherry Tomato (Prolific Crack-Resistant Sweet Cherry Tomato Hybrid)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Sweet Million is an indeterminate cherry tomato — provide a large cage or 6-foot stake as the vine grows very tall and continues producing through the entire season without stopping.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Sweet Million is an improved cherry tomato hybrid developed as an upgrade to the popular 'Sweet 100' — retaining the exceptional sweetness and massive productivity of Sweet 100 while adding significantly improved crack resistance that reduces the loss of ripe fruits during periods of uneven watering or heavy rainfall. The 1-inch, round, brilliant-red cherry tomatoes ripen in long clusters (trusses) of 15–25 fruits and have outstanding sweetness with good acidity — a classic cherry tomato flavor profile that is excellent for snacking, salads, and cooking. The crack-resistance improvement over Sweet 100 is practically important in Gulf South gardens where alternating dry periods and rainfall events cause significant cracking losses in thin-skinned cherry tomato varieties. Sweet Million produces enormous quantities of fruit on large, vigorous indeterminate vines that continue fruiting through the entire warm season in zone 9 — a single plant properly caged and fertilized can produce thousands of cherry tomatoes through the spring-to-fall production season. Cherry tomatoes as a class are more reliable through summer heat than large-fruited types and provide continuous production in zone 9 during the transition period between spring and fall planting windows when large-fruited varieties have ceased fruiting.

common_name: Sweet Olive Tomato (Very Sweet Small Oval Grape-Type Cherry Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Sweet Olive's thick skin and oval shape give it significantly better shelf life and handling durability than round cherry tomatoes — the firmer skin resists cracking and damage during harvest.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Sweet Olive is a grape-type cherry tomato — small (1–1.5 inch), oval to elongated, bright-red fruits with thick skin and meaty, low-moisture flesh that gives this type better handling durability, shelf life, and crack resistance than round cherry tomatoes. The grape-type classification refers to the oval, elongated shape resembling table grapes rather than the classic round cherry tomato form. The flavor is very sweet and concentrated — the firm, meaty flesh with lower water content intensifies the sweetness-per-bite relative to juicier round types. Sweet Olive produces large trusses of fruit on vigorous indeterminate vines. The thick skin and oval shape make grape-type tomatoes like Sweet Olive particularly practical for container growing, patio harvesting, and fresh snacking — the fruits don't burst when squeezed gently and hold their quality well at room temperature for several days after harvest. In zone 9, grape-type cherry tomatoes are among the most reliable summer producers because the thick skin provides some protection against cracking from uneven moisture that affects large-fruited and thin-skinned types. An excellent choice for continuous production through the warm season in zone 9 gardens.

common_name: Dixie Golden Giant Tomato (Very Large Golden-Yellow Southern Heirloom Beefsteak)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Dixie Golden Giant has been maintained and distributed through southern seed saving networks as a locally-adapted golden variety that performs reliably in Gulf South heat.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Dixie Golden Giant is a southern heirloom beefsteak tomato producing very large (1–2+ lb), round to slightly flattened fruits in a golden-yellow to deep-yellow color with exceptionally sweet, very low-acid, meaty flesh that is characteristic of the finest golden beefsteak types. The very large size and golden-yellow color combine to make Dixie Golden Giant one of the most visually impressive golden tomatoes — the large yellow fruits sliced on a plate have a beautiful, rich golden color and the thick, dense flesh produces substantial slices without excess seeding or gel. The flavor is typical of the best large yellow heirlooms: very sweet, very mild, fruity, and low in acid — appealing to those who find standard red tomatoes too sharp or acidic. As a southern-adapted heirloom that has been selected and maintained in Gulf South gardens, Dixie Golden Giant may show somewhat better adaptation to zone 9 conditions than northern golden beefsteak types — though all very large beefsteak tomatoes are challenging to grow through Gulf South summer heat and produce best in the spring growing season (February–June). An open-pollinated variety with saveable seed that grows true-to-type from season to season.

common_name: Knuckle Purple Hull Pea (Short-Season Compact Purple Hull Southern Pea)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when soil has warmed above 65°F in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Knuckle Purple Hull is a semi-determinate type that concentrates its pod set in a shorter period than indeterminate types — useful for gardeners who want a defined harvest window rather than continuous production.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost when soil is warm in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Knuckle Purple Hull is a semi-determinate purple hull southern pea that matures faster and concentrates its pod set in a shorter window than standard indeterminate purple hull types — producing the characteristic purple-hulled pods on a compact, upright plant that reaches 18–24 inches without trailing vines, making it more manageable for raised-bed and intensive garden production. The name 'Knuckle' refers to the distinctive bunching habit of pod production — the pods tend to cluster near the stem joints ('knuckles') in groups rather than being spread along extended vining stems, which concentrates the harvest and makes picking more efficient. The peas themselves are the classic purple hull type: green hulls with purple coloring at maturity, and cream-colored seeds with the mild, sweet flavor characteristic of purple hull peas. At 55–65 days, Knuckle Purple Hull is also earlier than standard purple hull types (typically 65–75 days) — allowing earlier first harvests from spring plantings and more productive use of the warm season in zone 9 where succession planting through summer is common practice. The compact, determinate-tending growth habit requires no staking and makes Knuckle Purple Hull suitable for higher-density plantings than trailing types.
